coredata.dc:title: Tea biomass carbon quantum dots mediated Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets for enhanced photocatalytic activities: Promoting the activation of molecular oxygen
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yanbing
Zhou, Ting
Li, Mingyang
Ye, Meng
Yi, Jianjian
Gu, Binxian
Hu, Qingsong
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, the development of catalysts with high spatial separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers and molecular oxygen activation has received extensive attention. In this research, a simple solvothermal method was employed to prepare Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets modified with different concentrations of tea biomass carbon quantum dots (T-BCDs), and the morphology, structure and photo-electrochemical characteristics of the as-prepared catalysts were explored in detail. The tea biomass quantum dots adopted a green synthesis strategy. The method converts waste into high-value biomass carbon quantum dots, thereby enabling sustainable use of resources. T-BCDs can store and transfer photogenerated electrons, which can accelerate photoinduced carrier migration and charge separation, boost molecular oxygen activation, and enhance photocatalytic activities. Among which, Bi12O17Cl2 loading with 2 mL T-BCDs displays significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance towards the removal of antibiotics under visible and near-infrared light irradiation. In addition, microbial toxicological experiments show that the toxicity of the degradation products is significantly reduced. Superoxide radicals (•O2 -) and holes (h+) are determined to be the major reactive species in photocatalytic reactions. Finally, a viable photocatalytic mechanism is put forward. This study provides a promising strategy for the development of wide-spectrum responsive catalyst in the field of environmental purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi12O17Cl2
Biomass carbon
Photocatalytic
Charge separation
Molecular oxygen activation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was \uFB01nancially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (NO. 22KJB610026), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (NO. 2021M691389) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (SJCX24_2260).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the formation of T-BCDs, Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite, (b) XRD patterns, (c) FT-IR spectra of T-BCDs, Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite.
(a) XPS C 1 s spectra of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite, XPS spectra of Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite: (b) Bi 4 f, (c) O 1 s, (d) Cl 2p.
SEM image of pure Bi12O17Cl2 (a), TEM image of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 (b), HRTEM images of pure Bi12O17Cl2 (c) and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 (d).

Photocatalytic performance of the catalysts for the degradation of TC (a), the corresponding reaction kinetics for the degradation of TC (d). Photocatalytic performance of the catalysts under visible light irradiation (λ＞400 nm) (b) and near-infrared light irradiation (λ＞610 nm) (c) for the degradation of CIP, the corresponding reaction kinetics for the degradation of CIP under visible light irradiation (λ＞400 nm) (e), and near-infrared light irradiation (λ＞610 nm) (f).

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite, (b) bandgap width of Bi12O17Cl2, (c) XPS valence-band spectra of Bi12O17Cl2, (d) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, (e) transient photocurrent and (f) photoluminescence of Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2.
(a) DMPO-•O2 -, (b) DMPO-•OH of Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2, (c) effect of trapping agent on tetracycline degradation, (d) band structure of Bi12O17Cl2.
Photocatalytic mechanism of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite in the degradation process.

head.ce:title: Tea biomass carbon quantum dots mediated Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets for enhanced photocatalytic activities: Promoting the activation of molecular oxygen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, 196 West Huayang Road, Yangzhou 225127, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, 196 West Huayang Road, Yangzhou, 225127, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Solid Organic Waste Resource Utilization, Nanjing 210095, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Solid Organic Waste Resource Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Solid Organic Waste Resource Utilization, Nanjing, 210095, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, the development of catalysts with high spatial separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers and molecular oxygen activation has received extensive attention. In this research, a simple solvothermal method was employed to prepare Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets modified with different concentrations of tea biomass carbon quantum dots (T-BCDs), and the morphology, structure and photo-electrochemical characteristics of the as-prepared catalysts were explored in detail. The tea biomass quantum dots adopted a green synthesis strategy. The method converts waste into high-value biomass carbon quantum dots, thereby enabling sustainable use of resources. T-BCDs can store and transfer photogenerated electrons, which can accelerate photoinduced carrier migration and charge separation, boost molecular oxygen activation, and enhance photocatalytic activities. Among which, Bi12O17Cl2 loading with 2 mL T-BCDs displays significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance towards the removal of antibiotics under visible and near-infrared light irradiation. In addition, microbial toxicological experiments show that the toxicity of the degradation products is significantly reduced. Superoxide radicals (•O2 -) and holes (h+) are determined to be the major reactive species in photocatalytic reactions. Finally, a viable photocatalytic mechanism is put forward. This study provides a promising strategy for the development of wide-spectrum responsive catalyst in the field of environmental purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Drug compounds such as antibiotics are one of the main sources of water pollution [1]. As a kind of special drugs, antibiotics are employed to prevent infections in humans and animals and are widely used in hospitals, pharmaceutical industry and agriculture [2,3]. Due to poor metabolism, antibiotics are easy to accumulate in aquatic environment, which has a serious impact on the health of humans and animals and damages the balance of the ecosystem [4,5]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop effective methods to degrade antibiotics in water bodies. Traditional wastewater treatment methods, such as physical treatment method, chemical processing method and biological treatment method, require long cycle, high energy consumption and low efficiency. As a control, photocatalytic technology can take advantage of solar energy to generate various reactive oxygen species (ROS), and degrade organic contaminants into CO2 and H2O [6,7]. However, traditional photocatalysts, such as TiO2 and ZnO, are limited to the poor photocatalytic efficiency due to their low solar energy utilization efficiency, slow photogenerated carrier separation rate and fast charge carrier recombination rate [7]. Therefore, it is of great importance to improve the utilization of solar photons and boost the spatial separation and migration of photoinduced carriers.

For decades, sillén-structured bismuth oxyhalide [BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I)] have got lots of attention, thanks to their unique layered structure [8]. They have been used in photocatalytic energy conversion and environmental remediation [9]. As a representative catalyst, BiOCl is consist of [Bi2O2]2+ layers and alternating two [Cl]- layers. The electrostatic field perpendicular to the [Bi2O2]2+ and [Cl]- layers can promote the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, but the wide band gap inhibits its overall photocatalytic performance [10,11]. Research in the past decade has shown that the photocatalytic performance can be improved through defect engineering and element doping [12,13]. Additionally, the photocatalytic activity can be improved by combining bismuth oxyhalides with graphene. Allagui et al.[14] prepared shell/core rGO/BiOBr composites via hydrothermal method to achieve effective charge carrier separation. rGO (1 %)/BiOBr displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity in the degradation of Orange II and acetaminophen. More than that, the bismuth-rich strategy, through adjusting the proportions of Bi, O and Cl to reduce the band gap and broaden the absorption region of visible light [15]. BixOyClz with increased “Bi” content can even promote the reduction ability of photoinduced electrons, thus triggering many reduction reactions that BiOCl cannot carry out [10]. Up to now, a variety of bismuth-rich bismuth oxychloride catalysts have been prepared, such as Bi24O31Cl10 [16], Bi12O15Cl6 [17],Bi12O17Cl2 [18] and so on. Among which, Bi12O17Cl2 has attracted much attention because of its stable chemical properties, wide-spectrum response, suitable energy band structure and non-toxicity [10,19]. Bi12O17Cl2 displays great potential in wastewater treatment, but the high photoexcited carrier recombination rate is still an important factor in limiting the photocatalytic efficiency [20]. Therefore, reasonable strategies should be adopted to improve the photocatalytic performance of Bi12O17Cl2.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials [21]. It is found that CQDs can not only reduce the recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes, but also broaden the visible-light absorption range of the catalyst [22]. For the past few years, biomass materials which served as carbon sources for the preparation of CQDs have drawn wide attention due to their advantages of low raw material costs and efficient carbon recycling [23,24]. Biomass such as peanut shell, tea residue, bagasse and rice residue are often used as initial carbon sources for the preparation of CQDs [25]. Compared with other quantum dots, biomass carbon quantum dots are more environmentally friendly and have the potential to decrease toxicity and biocompatibility [26]. The carbon dots synthesized by biomass sources also reduce photoinduced carrier recombination while broadening visible-light absorption range. Li et al. employed withered magnolia waste biomass as a carbon source to obtain CoAl-LDH (B/CAL) catalyst with biomass carbon quantum dots interleaved. Their findings demonstrated that the introduction of biomass carbon quantum dots enhances light absorption and improves the separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [27]. Therefore, biomass carbon quantum dots can be used to further enhance photocatalytic activities.

In this work, tea was employed as carbon source to prepare a biomass carbon quantum dots (T-BCDs) and then modified Bi12O17Cl2 photocatalyst via solvothermal method. The microstructure and chemical composition of the as-prepared samples were studied by various characterization methods. At the same time, the photocatalytic activities of the samples were studied via the degradation of tetracycline (TC) and ciprofloxacin (CIP). The results showed that the degradation performance of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 was significantly higher than that of single Bi12O17Cl2. The introduction of biomass carbon quantum dots can significantly boost the separation of photoinduced carriers and thus enormously enhance photocatalytic performance. Finally, the band structure and possible photocatalytic mechanism is proposed by radicals quenching tests, ESR spectra analysis, Mott-Schottky curves and XPS valence band. This research offers a novel perspective on the search for effective semiconductor photocatalysts in environmental remediation.

In this investigation, each chemical reagent used was analytically pure. The reagents used are listed below. Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3•5 H2O, AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), Ethylene glycol (EG) (Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd.), polyvinyl-pyrrolidone (PVP-K30, Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.).

0.50 g tea powder was weighed and dispersed into 50 mL deionized water. After ultrasonic treatment for 20 min, the mixed solution was transferred into a 100 mL autoclave lined with Teflon, heated at 200 °C for 12 h. Finally, the product was dialyzed for 24 h (retained molecular weight: 500 Da). During the dialysis process, a magnetic stirrer was used to keep the water in the beaker in a flowing state, and the dialysis water was changed every 6 h. T-BCDs solution was then obtained. The concentration of T-BCDs solution was about 2 mg/mL.

0.97 g Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O was added into 10 mL ethylene glycol by ultrasonic treatment and defined as solution A. 0.32 g NH4Cl and 0.80 g NaOH were added into deionized water and defined as solution B (pH=13.5). Solution A was added dropwise into solution B while stirring. 0.40 g polyvinyl-pyrrolidone (PVP-K30) was weighed and added into the above mixture. The mixture was stirred for 1 h and transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and allowed to react for 12 h at 140 °C. When the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the precipitate was collected by high speed centrifugal separation, and washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol. After drying, the sample was obtained.

2 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O was added into 10 mL ethylene glycol by ultrasonic treatment and defined as solution A. Solution B was acquired by adding 0.32 g of NH4Cl, 0.80 g of NaOH, and X mL of T-BCDs (X = 1, 2 and 3) into 60 mL deionized water. Solution A was added dropwise to solution B by contant stirring. 0.40 g PVP-K30 was added into the aforementioned mixed solution. The mixture was stirred for 1 h and then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, reacted at 140 °C for 12 h. When the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the precipitate was collected by high speed centrifugal separation, and washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol. The final products were obtained after drying. The as-prepared samples were labeled as T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2–1, T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-3 on account of the increasing addition amount of T-BCDs.

The crystal structure of the obtained catalysts was investigated by X-ray diffractometer with monochromatic Cu Kα radiation (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi, ThermoFisher, USA) was utilized to investigate the catalysts’ surface chemical state and elemental composition. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7800F, JEOL, Japan)) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, USA) were used to analyze the surface morphology and microstructure of the catalysts. The surface functional groups of the samples were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, NEXUS 670, Nicolet, USA). The UV–visible spectroscopy was recorded by UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu, Japan) in the scanning range of 200–800 nm. The specific surface and aperture analyzer (Quadrasorb EVO, Anton Paar, USA) was used to explore the specific surface area and pore diameter distribution. Additionally, a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer was used to study the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the catalysts. A JES-FA200 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) was utilized to gather the electron spin resonance (ESR) signals. A conventional three-electrode system was used for electrochemical analysis in an electrochemical workstation (DH7006, Donghua Instrument Company, China).

In this study, TC and CIP were employed to assess the photocatalytic performance of the acquired samples. The light source was a 250 W xenon lamp fitted with an optical filter (λ > 400 nm) and an intensity of 100 mW/cm2. The circulating cooling water system was carried out to keep the reaction temperature constant. For antibiotic degradation experiments, 50 mg samples were added into 100 mL TC solution (20 mg/L), or 80 mg samples were added into 100 mL CIP solution (10 mg/L). The suspensions were magnetically agitated in the darkness for 0.5 h to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After turning on the xenon light, 4.5 mL suspension was taken at every interval of 0.5 h and the supernatant was obtained by centrifugation. The obtained solution was determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometer according to the absorbance wavelength at 357 nm and 276 nm respectively.

T-BCDs were prepared from tea powder by a simple hydrothermal method, and the target substance was obtained by dialysis. T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite were acquired by PVP assisted solvothermal method [28,29]. As a kind of encapsulation agent, PVP is easy to form nanocomposite catalyst and improve the stability of nanoparticles [30]. In this work, we synthesized pure Bi12O17Cl2 and a series of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite with different T-BCDs loading capacities. Based on the amount of T-BCDs added, the nanocomposite catalyst is named as T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-X (X = 1, 2, 3). The synthetic procedure is shown in Fig. 1a.

XRD was used to study the crystal structure of Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 1b, the 2θ values of 23.2°, 24.3°, 26.4°, 29.2°, 30.4°, 32.9°, 45.5°, 47.2° and 56.4° correspond to different crystallographic planes of Bi12O17Cl2, namely (111), (113), (115), (117), (0012), (200), (2012), (220), and (317). All diffraction peaks can correspond to the diffraction peaks of tetragonal Bi12O17Cl2 (JCPDS No. 37–0702) and no glaring impurity peaks can be detected, which indicates that Bi12O17Cl2 has been successfully prepared [21]. In addition, the peaks and lattice parameters of the nanocomposite are similar to those of Bi12O17Cl2. Nevertheless, there are no discernible characteristic peaks of T-BCDs, which may be explained by the low loading capacity, small nanometer size and high dispersion of T-BCDs. With the increasing addition amount of T-BCDs, the diffraction peaks of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite are slightly enhanced. But the influence on the width of the diffraction peaks is weak, which indicates that most of T-BCDs are uniformly covered on the surface of Bi12O17Cl2 and do not enter the lattice [22].

The molecular structure of the catalysts was analyzed by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1c, the characteristic peaks of Bi12O17Cl2 are concentrated at 551 cm−1, 849 cm−1, and 1386 cm−1. These peaks can match well with the tensile vibration in Bi-O bond, the bending vibration in O-Bi-O bond and the asymmetric tensile vibration in Bi-Cl bond, respectively [15,31]. After the formation of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite, these characteristic peaks shift to 854 cm−1 and 1415 cm−1. This can be attributed to the interaction between T-BCDs and Bi12O17Cl2 [32]. The characteristic peak of T-BCDs at 1642 cm−1 can correspond to the presence of CC [33]. FT-IR results demonstrate that T-BCDs has been successfully introduced into Bi12O17Cl2 nanoparticle.

The chemical state of each element in the as-prepared catalysts was analyzed by XPS. Fig. S1 displays a full spectrum of the samples, which manifests that the main elements are C, Bi, O, and Cl. As depicted in Fig. 2a, the peak at 284.80 eV can be ascribed to the C-C bond with sp2 orbital, while CC and CO were assigned to the peaks located at 285.85 and 288.06 eV, respectively [34]. In Fig. 2b, the two prominent peaks centered at 158.40 and 163.70 eV are associated with Bi 4 f7/2 and Bi 4 f5/2, respectively, indicating that Bi exists in the highest oxidation state [35]. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s is displayed in Fig. 2c. The two peaks at the binding energies of 528.98 and 530.62 eV can be assigned to the lattice oxygen (Bi-O) and hydroxyl species (O-H), respectively [36,37]. In Fig. 2 d, the characteristic peaks with the binding energies at 197.62 and 199.25 eV are attributed to Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2. Moreover, the shift of binding energy in T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 can further confirm the existence of electron transfer between T-BCDs and Bi12O17Cl2. It is generally accepted that the binding energy increases with the loss of electrons [38]. Along with the introduction of T-BCDs, the binding energies of Bi 4 f, O 1 s, and Cl 2p improves, which demonstrates that the surface charge transfers between T-BCDs and Bi12O17Cl2. This can be explained that the direction of electron transfers from Bi12O17Cl2 to T-BCDs. The shift in binding energy also clearly reveals electron redistribution between Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs [34,39].

TEM, HRTEM, and SEM characterizations were used to study the microstructure and morphology of the catalysts. SEM image of pure Bi12O17Cl2 is shown in Fig. 3a. Bi12O17Cl2 displays a typical nanosheet structure. As can be seen from Fig. 3b, the modification of T-BCDs poses no obvious effect on the morphology of Bi12O17Cl2. And T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite presents nanosheet structure. The microstructure of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite was further studied by HRTEM analysis. Fig. S3a displays the HRTEM image of T-BCDs. It can be seen from the figure that the size of T-BCDs is about 3–5 nm. In addition, Fig. S3b shows the T-BCDs are evenly distributed on the surface of Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets. In Fig. 3c, the lattice spacing is about 0.27 nm, corresponding to (200) crystal plane of Bi12O17Cl2. It can be seen from Fig. 3d, the lattice of both Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs can be observed simultaneously. Among which, the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is attributed to (110) crystal plane of T-BCDs [40,41]. The above characterization results uncover the close interface contact between T-BCDs and Bi12O17Cl2, which favors the transporation of photoinduced carriers.

BET measurements were carried out to investigate the surface properties of the as-prepared samples. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 are displayed in Fig. S3. The specific surface area by BET model are calculated to be 14.7296 m2·g−1 and 15.5037 m2·g−1, respectively. After the addition of T-BCDs, the specific surface area of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite has no obvious change. It can be asserted that the specific surface area is not the key factor in determining photocatalytic efficiency in this system. In addition, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms reveal that the two catalysts display type-IV curve, implying that they belong to mesoporous structure. It should be pointed out that the construction of mesoporous structures is advantageous for enhancing photocatalytic performance, demonstrating considerable potential in the field of photocatalysis [42].

TC was employed as the target pollutant to evaluate the photocatalytic activities of Bi12O17Cl2 and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite. As depicted in Fig. 4a, the concentration of TC varies little under illumination without the introduction of catalyst.

The self-degradation is almost negligible. And the degradation efficiency of pure Bi12O17Cl2 is poor. Only about 37.8 % of TC can be degraded after illumination for 180 min. In contrast, T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite displays enhanced photocatalytic performance. Among which, more than 76.2 % of TC can be degraded over T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2. To further compare the degradation rates, the pseudo-first-order kinetic model is introduced. The reaction rate constant (k) can be obtained by the following formula: -ln (C/C0) = kt. The higher the k value, the faster the reaction rate and the greater the degradation efficiency. In Fig. 4d, it can be clearly observed that T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2–2 possesses the greatest reaction rate constant (0.0125 min−1), which is about 4.46, 1.23 and 1.36 times higher than those of Bi12O17Cl2 (0.0028 min−1), T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-1 (0.0102 min−1) and T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2–3 (0.0110 min−1). It is amazing to find that the photocatalytic efficiency improves with the loading amount of T-BCDs. Nevertheless, when the loading amount is over 2 mL, the photocatalytic efficiency decreases obviously. This may be explained that excessive T-BCDs cover around the catalyst, hindering the surface-active sites, resulting in poor catalytic activity.

To further investigate the degradation performance over other types of antibiotics, CIP was also employed as a target contaminant. CIP exists widely in a variety of water environments, poses potential threats to aquatic organisms [43]. Thus, research on the elimination of CIP is crucial. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the self-degradation of CIP is negligible under illumination. More than 81.7 % of CIP can be removed over T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 within 180 min, much higher than that of Bi12O17Cl2. Additionally, the reaction kinetics of CIP degradation was studied. In Fig. 4e, the reaction rate constant of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 is about 0.0163 min−1, which is almost 3.20 times higher than that of Bi12O17Cl2 (0.0051 min−1). Meanwhile, the near-infrared light experiment was carried out to study the photocatalytic performance. A 610 nm filter is employed in the photocatalytic experiment, allowing near-infrared light to pass through.

In Fig. 4c, T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 can degrade about 69.3 % of CIP under near-infrared light irradiation, much higher than that of Bi12O17Cl2. The corresponding reaction rate constant is 0.0104 min−1, about 1.73 times higher than that of Bi12O17Cl2 (0.0060 min−1) (Fig. 4f). The aforementioned experimental results demonstrate that T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite can retain preferable photocatalytic efficiency under visible and near-infrared light illumination. Based on the photocatalytic degradation experiments, it is feasible to infer that the addition of T-BCDs can considerably optimize the photocatalytic activities of Bi12O17Cl2.

It is a key factor to keep stable and reusable features in the catalytic reactions. Because it is related to the lifetime and catalytic efficiency of the catalyst. To evaluate the stability and reusability of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite, cycling experiments were performed under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. S4a, there is no obvious variation in the degradation efficiency of TC, which indicate that T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite possesses excellent reusable performance. To further confirm the stability of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite, a number of characterization experiments were conducted. From the XRD pattern (Fig. S4b), it can be noted that the crystal variation of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite before and after the catalytic reactions is negligible. At the same time, the XPS spectra (Fig. S5) shows that the characteristic peaks of the cycled catalysts can be matched well with the original catalyst. Based on the above analysis, T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite displays satisfactory stability.

In order to investigate the toxicity of intermediates generated by T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 in the process of antibiotic degradation, ecotoxicity experiments were carried out employing Escherichia coli (Fig. S6). After 24 h, the number of colonies on the agar plate show that the colony activity of the degraded solution by T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 is significantly higher than that of the original solution. This implies that the toxicity of the intermediate products can be effectively reduced. This finding shows significant ramifications for the practical application of eliminating antibiotics.

A number of optical and electrochemical experiments were conducted to study the intrinsic reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic activity. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was used to analyze the optical absorption capacity. As indicated in Fig. 5a, T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite exhibits greater light absorption intensity than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2. The wider visible-light absorption range is more beneficial to make full use of solar energy and generate more carriers, which is favor of improving photocatalytic activity [21]. At the same time, the bandgap width is calculated by classical Tauc approach. The curves of (αhυ)2 and hυ are plotted based on the formula αhυ = A(hυ−E g)n/2 [44,45]. It can be observed from Fig. 5b that the bandgap width of Bi12O17Cl2 is 1.87 eV. Meanwhile, the band structure of the sample was evaluated by Mott-Schottky and XPS valence band spectrum. The flat band potential of the sample was determined via Mott-Schottky curves. According to Fig. S7, the slope of the Mott-Schottky curves are positive, demonstrating that Bi12O17Cl2 is a n-type semiconductor [46]. The flat-band potential of Bi12O17Cl2 is about −1.09 V vs Ag/AgCl (pH = 7), which is equal to −0.89 V vs NHE (pH = 7). Previous researches have shown that the fermi level of n-type semiconductor is similar to the flat-band potential [19,47]. In addition, the XPS valence band spectrum was utilized to measure the gap between the fermi level and the potential of the valence band in the sample. As shown in Fig. 5c, the highest energy edge of the valence band (VB) is 1.55 eV. As a result, the valence band of Bi12O17Cl2 is determined to be 0.66 V vs NHE. In addition, the CB of Bi12O17Cl2 can be calculated to be −1.21 V vs NHE on the account of E VB = E g + E CB [48].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to study the separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers. The arc radius of EIS Nyquist plots are closely related to the carrier separation efficiency [49]. The arc radius reflects the electron transfer obstruction of the catalyst, and the decrease of arc radius indicates the enhancement of carrier separation efficiency [50]. Fig. 5d shows that T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 possesses a smaller resistance, which manifests that the addition of T-BCDs can effectively improve the separation capacity of photoinduced carriers. In addition, the carrier separation efficiency of the as-prepared catalysts can be further analyzed by the photocurrent response analysis. In order to obtain the photoresponse characteristics of the obtained catalysts, their photocurrent behavior was examined. In general, the intensity of the photocurrent is positively associated with the efficient electrons and holes in the redox reaction [51]. It is evident from Fig. 5e that the photocurrent intensity of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 is significantly higher than that of Bi12O17Cl2. Therefore, T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 displays higher photoinduced carrier separation efficiency, which is consistent with EIS analysis. The recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs can also be assessed by photoluminescence (PL). Since photons are generated when electrons are ejected from CB and subsequently recombine with holes. The intensity of PL increases after photogenerated electrons and holes recombine, resulting in lower photocatalytic activity [52]. As depicted in Fig. 5f, the emission peak is located at around 470 nm. And the peak height of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite is obviously lower than that of Bi12O17Cl2. This indicates that the photoinduced carrier recombination rate of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite is lower than that of Bi12O17Cl2. The results of photo-electrochemical experiments are in accord with the photocatalytic degradation data. It is feasible to draw the conclusion that the addition of T-BCDs can boost the separation and transporation efficiency of photoinduced carriers, which results in enhanced photocatalytic activities.

To explore the major reactive species involved into the degradation of antibiotics, electron spin resonance (ESR) technique was carried out. 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was utilized as the radical scavenger. As can be seen from Fig. 6a, the characteristic signal of DMPO-•O2 - can not be detected in the darkness. Surprisingly, the signal peak of DMPO-•O2 - can be detected under illumination, which indicates that •O2 - is the main active species during the degradation process. And the DMPO-•O2 - signal intensity of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 is significantly stronger than that of Bi12O17Cl2. Considering that •O2 - is generated by one-electron reduction of molecular oxygen, the addition of T-BCDs can promote photoinduced electrons involved into the molecule oxygen activation. On the contrary, the DMPO-•OH signal peaks can not be detected in the darkness or under illumination in Fig. 6b. It is confirmed that •OH is not the primary active species. ESR analysis preliminarily indicate that •O2 - is the primary active species in the degradation process of contaminants.

Free radical quenching tests were performed to achieve a better understanding of the active species involved into the photocatalytic process (Fig. 6c). The hole (h+) was captured by ammonium oxalate (AO), while •OH was captured by tert-butanol (TBA). In addition, argon was used to remove oxygen, thereby preventing the production of •O2 - [53]. When TBA is added into the reaction solution, the degradation efficiency retain stable. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency decreases significantly after AO is added. The results showed that •OH did not participate in the degradation of pollutants, and h+ played a key role in the degradation process. In addition, the photocatalytic activity decreased significantly after Ar was introduced, indicating that •O2 - were significantly involved in the process. The results of free radical quenching are corresponding to those of ESR experiment. In the meantime, the photocatalytic mechanism can be identified according to the band structure of Bi12O17Cl2. According to the previous calculation, the valence band potential of Bi12O17Cl2 is 0.66 V. It can be inferred that E0(•OH/OH-) = 2.38 V is more positive than Evb and Bi12O17Cl2 cannot oxidize H2O into •OH [54]. However, since Bi12O17Cl2 has a greater negative CB potential (-1.21 V) than O2/•O2 - potential (-0.046 V), its electrons have the ability to reduce O2 to •O2 -. The analysis presented above are in line with the results of ESR analysis and free radical capture test. The band structure of Bi12O17Cl2 is shown in Fig. 6d.

Based on the findings and band structure analysis, the photocatalytic mechanism of T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2 nanocomposite for TC and CIP is proposed and shown in Fig. 7. Under visible light irradiation, electrons transition from the VB of Bi12O17Cl2 to the CB, forming photoinduced electrons in the CB and photoinduced holes in the VB. The photogenerated carriers continuously migrate to the surface of the catalyst. Strong oxidation capability superoxide radicals are created when oxygen is stimulated by electrons. As the primary active species, •O2 - and h+ participated in the degradation of antibiotics. After modifying Bi12O17Cl2 with T-BCDs, photoinduced electrons can migrate to T-BCDs and be captured by surface-adsorbed oxygen molecules to form superoxide radicals, which can decompose organic contaminants into small molecules. In this process, T-BCDs plays a role in the storage and transfer of electrons, which can effectively drive the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Therefore, the recombination of photogenerated carriers is inhibited and the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation is improved. In general, •O2 - and h+ are generated under illumination, which together participate in the removal of antibiotics. As a medium for storing and transferring electrons, the introduction of T-BCDs effectively improves the utilization of photoinduced carriers and generates more •O2 -. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance is improved.

To sum up, T-BCDs modified Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets were successfully obtained to realize the spatial separation and transport of photogenerated carriers. The special delocalization structure of T-BCDs can boost the separation of photogenerated electron and hole pairs. More photoinduced electrons can be participated into the reduction of molecular oxygen. However, excessive T-BCDs coating around the catalyst may hinder the surface-active sites, resulting in poor catalytic activity. Therefore, T-BCDs/Bi12O17Cl2-2 shows the best photocatalytic performance, and its kinetic rate constant for TC and CIP is calculated to be 0.0125 and 0.0163 min−1, which are 4.46 and 3.20 times higher than that of Bi12O17Cl2. According to ESR and free radical quenching tests, it can be inferred that superoxide radicals and holes play a crucial part in photocatalytic process. In addition, the nanocomposite photocatalyst displays preferable photocatalytic activity under near-infrared radiation (λ>610 nm), and is proved harmless to the environment on the basis of ecotoxicity test. Our research extends the knowledge into developing bismuth-rich catalysts modified by biomass carbon quantum dots with broad applications in the field of environmental purification.

Hu Qingsong: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Zhou Ting: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Liu Yanbing: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Ye Meng: Data curation. Li Mingyang: Investigation, Data curation. Gu Binxian: Supervision. Yi Jianjian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was ﬁnancially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (NO. 22KJB610026), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (NO. 2021M691389) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (SJCX24_2260).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.115992.
